titleStmt.title: The Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive

respStmt.orgName: University of South Carolina Provost Humanities Grants
University of South Carolina Department of English
Center for Digital Humanities at the University of South Carolina
Magellan Scholar Program at the University of South Carolina
National Society of the Daughters of the American Revolution
Carnegie-Whitney Grant from the American Library Association

availability.p: Periodicals and books published before 1 January 1923 are in the public domain. If re-publishing, quoting from, or reproducing images from this digital archive, we request that you cite it as follows: Lone Woman and Last Indians Digital Archive, Center for Digital Humanities, University of South Carolina.

Periodicals and books published on or after 1 January 1923 may be protected by U.S. Copyright Law. All attempts to secure permission for republishing post-1923 material in this archive have been made. The names, logos, and identifying images, as well as the content of the articles, remain the property of the periodicals and/or of the authors and artists themselves. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder. Archival material published in this archive is used with permission of the institution or library that holds it. Further reproduction, as well as quotation beyond fair use, is prohibited; permission must be obtained from the copyright holder.
Responsibility for any use of the contents of this digital archive rests exclusively with the user.

bibl.title: Historic Facts and Fancies: History and Landmarks Section of California Federated Women's Clubs
Lost Woman of San Nicolas Island

rs.persName: Lost Woman of San Nicholas Island

div.byline: (Some extracts from an article in Overland Monthly, May 1896, by L. G. Yates, of Santa Barbara.)
div.p: SAN NICHOLAS ISLAND, one of the group of the Santa Barbara Islands, is about nine miles in length and four in width; water is plentiful; was formerly densely populated, but now is a treeless waste—the result of fire and the pasturage of sheep.

We learn by tradition that the Alaska Indians, who were placed upon this island to trade with the natives, killed off the male inhabitants and took possession. In 1835 the padres sent a vessel to remove the women and children. This was accomplished, with the exception of one woman, who jumped into the surf and swam ashore to hunt for her child, who had accidentally been left behind.

The next we hear of the lone woman was in 1850, after a lapse of fifteen years., when Captain Nidever, of Santa Barbara, visited the islands and discovered traces of recent habitation; but it was not until 1853 that the female Robinson Crusoe was found and removed to the mainland, where from the sudden and complete change of manner of living, she soon died.She had outlived her people and found no one able to converse with her in her almost forgotten language. It is supposed she was the last of her race; her clothing, made from skins, and needles of bone, were deposited in the Vatican, at Rome.

p.span: But the incidents connected with her stay upon and removal from the island will soon be forgotten, although the refuse heaps of its former inhabitants will remain as evidence of a lost people.

div.p: Who can realize the utter and wretched loneliness of the poor creature, who spent eighteen years among the deserted homes of her ancestors, where the ground was whitened by their bleaching bones?
